summary: Martin Fowler explores the enduring but elusive goal of building software through component assembly rather than hand-crafted code. He adopts Ralph Johnson's customer-centric definition: components are things that are independently replaceable and upgradeable, allowing users to mix, match, and upgrade on their own schedule. Fowler identifies two modern forms of components: libraries (code linked into processes) and services (separate processes accessed via interprocess communication). The key distinction from ordinary modules is that components must allow independent replacement without forcing coordinated upgrades across the system.
key_points: Components are defined by independent replaceability and upgradeability, not by technology
The component vision serves customer needs: buying software piecemeal and upgrading on their own schedule
Libraries and services are the two modern manifestations of software components
Components are a specialized form of module with the additional property of independent replacement
For services to be true components, you should never need to coordinate upgrades between services
money_quotes: Components are not a technology. Technology people seem to find this hard to understand.
Components are about how customers want to relate to software.
They want to be able to buy their software a piece at a time, and to be able to upgrade it just like they can upgrade their stereo.
This is a very reasonable requirement. It is just hard to satisfy.
Software components are things that are independently replaceable and upgradeable.
For services to be components, you should never need to coordinate the upgrade of one service with another service.
key_insight: Software components are defined not by their technology but by their property of independent replaceability—enabling customers to upgrade and mix pieces on their own terms.
